title: Interactive GxE Textbook
text:
Welcome to the Interactive GxE Textbook
Explore quantitative stability frameworks, models, and transformations for Genotype-by-Environment (GxE) interactions.
This textbook replaces static chapters with a dynamic, interactive reading experience. Built with Quarto, it allows readers to engage directly with analytical plots, exploring the multidimensionality of GxE data.
crumbs: Welcome to the Interactive GxE Textbook

title: Interactive GxE Textbook
text:
Modules Overview

1. GxE Conceptualisation & Frameworks: Visualizing absolute performance vs. relative stability.
2. GxE Data Transformation Engine: Isolating interaction components from MET data.
3. Classical GxE Stability Compendium: Contrasting the mathematical logic behind stability metrics.

Select a chapter from the sidebar or click the links above to begin.
crumbs: Welcome to the Interactive GxE Textbook

title: 1  GxE Conceptualisation & Frameworks
text:
1.1 Performance Plot
Understanding Genotype-by-Environment interactions requires separating absolute performance from relative stability.
Emphasizes patterns of response across sites. The environment mean is used as a proxy index for absolute ecosystem productivity (Yates & Cochran / Finlay-Wilkinson).

title: 1  GxE Conceptualisation & Frameworks
text:
1.2 Discrimination Plot (Bar Charts)
Provides a visual ‘relative genotype performance index’ (Ivory et al. 1991). Highlights performance trends and how environments elicit unique variance.

title: 1  GxE Conceptualisation & Frameworks
text:
1.3 Proximity Plot (Ordination)
Spatial position displays performance similarity in strict Euclidean space. Closer distance = highly similar GxE profile. Discrepant distances = high rank instability.

1.3.1 Interactive Proximity Plot Demonstration
Below is an interactive demonstration of a 3D GxE Proximity Plot using simulated data. You can rotate, zoom, and hover to see individual genotype stabilities across environments.

source("../../ScriptBook/GxEscripts/GxE conceptualisation.R")







Figure 1.1: GxE Conceptualisation Plots

title: 2  GxE Data Transformation Engine
text:
Implementing structural data transformations allows us to isolate interaction components from raw multi-environment trial (MET) data:

1. Untransformed: Baseline raw data model capturing all phenotypic yield.
2. Environment Centred: Removes the main environmental effect (mean = 0) to observe pure deviation.
3. Environment Standardized: Scales by within-environment phenotypic variance to equalise heterogeneous trial quality.
4. Heritability Weighted: Shrunk by reliability coefficient. Scales by the square root of line-mean heritability.
5. Environment Ranked: Non-parametric proportions. Proportional mapping of ranks between 0 (worst) and 1 (best).


2.0.1 Interactive Transformation Comparison
Below is an interactive Plotly boxplot demonstrating how raw data distributions change across environments after applying the “Environment Standardized” transformation. Hover over the boxes to see quartile statistics.

source("../../ScriptBook/GxEscripts/GxE data transformation.R")

title: 3  Classical GxE Stability Compendium
text:
Contrasting mathematical logic behind stability parameters:

1. Regression Framework: Finlay-Wilkinson & Eberhart-Russell evaluate sensitivity slope (bi) and residual deviation (s2di). Racehorses show bi > 1, static types show bi -> 0.
2. Static Selection Window: Francis & Kannenberg use Coefficient of Variation (CV) vs. Mean. The ideal genotype exists in the top-left quadrant (high yield, low CV).
3. Additive Interaction Space: Wricke’s Ecovalence and Shukla’s Stability Variance measure identical structural deviations.
4. Superiority Index: Lin & Binns distance penalizes genotypes that drop below the maximum yield at any site, optimizing for distance to the frontier.


3.0.1 Interactive Static Selection Window
Below is an interactive Francis & Kannenberg Plot (CV vs Mean). The ideal genotypes fall into the top-left quadrant (high mean yield, low coefficient of variation). You can hover over points to identify specific genotypes.

source("../../ScriptBook/GxEscripts/GxE Stability Formulas.R")
